title: Exit Strategy

jokes.text: Title card reads 'It's... Attested Development'
jokes.setup: The show is called 'Arrested Development'; this episode involves legal depositions and testimony.
jokes.punchline: 'Attested' replaces 'Arrested' — a legal pun referencing the deposition storyline while winking at the show's title.
jokes.explanation: The title card pun is a signature AD move — dense, economical, and thematically tied to the episode. 'Attested' works on multiple levels (legal testimony, the show title, the act of being deposed). It rewards attentive viewers who catch it immediately. The pun is clever rather than groan-worthy, and it's earned because depositions are literally the episode's A-plot driver.

jokes.text: Lindsay, when asked how to respond to deposition questions about financial impropriety, says she'll 'unbutton my shirt a bit and give him this look.'
jokes.setup: Michael is coaching Lindsay on how to respond to serious legal deposition questions about financial crimes.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's instinctive strategy is to be seductive rather than address the substance — a perfect encapsulation of her vapid, self-obsessed character.
jokes.explanation: This is pure Lindsay characterization — her solution to every problem is to exploit her looks. It's economical and character-specific. High character integration score because this joke wouldn't work for any other character. Michael's baffled 'Not really sure what you're going for there' straight-man response lands it cleanly.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'If I look like a man who made love to his wife last night, it's because I almost did. That's why.'
jokes.setup: Tobias appears disheveled; Michael notices. Tobias offers an explanation implying sexual activity.
jokes.punchline: 'Almost did' — the boast collapses immediately. Also the redundant 'That's why' adds a layer of oblivious self-congratulation to a non-achievement.
jokes.explanation: Tobias's marriage is a running joke throughout the series — he and Lindsay have essentially no sexual relationship. The setup implies a normal marital boast, then 'almost' punctures it entirely, and the defiant 'That's why' shows Tobias has reframed 'almost' as an accomplishment. Pure Tobias: delusional, oblivious, and inadvertently honest about his failures.

jokes.text: Tobias explains finishing off the vodka: 'It goes bad once it's opened.' Michael: 'I think that's another one of Mom's little fibs. You know, like I'll sacrifice anything for my children.'
jokes.setup: Tobias says he had to finish the vodka because it 'goes bad once it's opened' — a fictional excuse.
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately identifies this as a Lucille myth alongside 'I'll sacrifice anything for my children' — a devastating character indictment of Lucille delivered as a throwaway aside.
jokes.explanation: The joke works on two levels: first, Tobias has been gaslit into believing vodka goes bad (he adopted Lucille's lies as facts); second, Michael's counterexample — that Lucille's self-proclaimed maternal sacrifice is equally fictional — is a sharp, earned character burn. The economy is excellent: one line does double work.

jokes.text: Tobias says he got a call to play 'a very important part in The Prosecution' and assumes it's a CBS procedural.
jokes.setup: The prosecutor has reached out to Tobias to get him to cooperate against the family.
jokes.punchline: Tobias hears 'The Prosecution' and thinks it's a CBS TV show, assuming he's been cast.
jokes.explanation: Tobias's actor delusion is one of the show's richest running character traits. The joke is layered: the prosecution literally wants him to testify, and Tobias processes the exact same information through an actor's ego. 'CBS procedural' is a specific, funny detail. His entire worldview filters reality through the lens of show business — this is the purest expression of that.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'Although they didn't send sides, so I thought I'd trot out a Vagina Monologue, or something else I know.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is preparing for what he thinks is an audition for a CBS legal drama.
jokes.punchline: He plans to perform 'The Vagina Monologue' — a piece entirely inappropriate for the context, demonstrating both his delusion and his obliviousness to gendered performance material.
jokes.explanation: The joke layers Tobias's actor desperation (any material is usable) with gender-obliviousness that the show has mined throughout. 'Or something else I know' is a great trailing qualifier — he's treating 'The Vagina Monologue' as just one of many pieces in his rotation.

jokes.text: Tobias says the prosecution 'asked for old photos and documents so I went and got these out of the secret room.'
jokes.setup: Michael is worried about evidence falling into the prosecution's hands.
jokes.punchline: Tobias has cheerfully retrieved the most incriminating possible documents from the family's own secret room, believing it's 'set dressing' for a TV show.
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony is rich: the audience knows exactly what those documents contain and what handing them over means. Tobias is unwittingly destroying the family's legal defense while feeling helpful. 'For set dressing, silly' is the perfect capper — he's not just wrong, he's condescendingly wrong.

jokes.text: Michael: 'The prosecution is not a TV show.' Tobias: 'These guys will bend the law to enforce the law. Tell me that's not a CBS franchise.'
jokes.setup: Michael tries to explain that 'The Prosecution' is a real legal proceeding, not a TV show.
jokes.punchline: Tobias immediately reframes Michael's actual description of prosecutorial overreach as a compelling TV logline.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's standout jokes. Michael inadvertently pitches a TV show while explaining reality, and Tobias catches it perfectly. 'Tell me that's not a CBS franchise' is quotable, specific, and lands as both a character joke AND a sharp media satire. The structure is excellent: Michael provides the setup without knowing it, and Tobias delivers the punchline. High craft score is justified — it's layered, economical, and only Tobias could say this line.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'These Hollywood shows are so incredibly detailed.' / 'For set dressing, silly.' — re: why prosecutors want old documents
jokes.setup: Michael explains the prosecution wants the family documents; Tobias handed them over
jokes.punchline: Tobias cheerfully explains he gave incriminating documents to prosecutors because he thought they were set designers
jokes.explanation: The escalation of Tobias's delusion — it's not just that he misidentified the call, he actively handed over the family's secret documents. 'For set dressing, silly' is perfectly pitched Tobias condescension toward anyone questioning his show business expertise. Rewatch bonus because earlier we see him retrieve the documents.

jokes.text: Michael's non-response when Tobias asks 'Including me?' about the prosecution coming after the family, followed by Michael awkwardly pivoting to Maeby's birthday.
jokes.setup: Michael warns everyone the prosecution will come after them. Tobias, who has no real secrets (or so everyone thinks), asks if he's included.
jokes.punchline: Michael's 'Hmm? No. You don't have any secrets.' pause, then Tobias's sudden 'Um, hey, speaking of that...' pivot to Maeby's birthday — implying he does have secrets Michael doesn't know about.
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the gap: Tobias's deflection implies hidden secrets, played as a quick beat. The 'speaking of that' pivot to a completely unrelated topic is a classic comedic misdirection. Works better on rewatch when you know what Tobias might be hiding. The awkward transition is the joke.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Actually, he'd already told her about it.' — immediately after Michael says he's planning a 'surprise' party for Maeby.
jokes.setup: Michael enthusiastically proposes throwing Maeby a surprise party to make her feel included.
jokes.punchline: The narrator immediately reveals Michael already told Maeby about it, deflating the entire premise before it's even established.
jokes.explanation: The narrator as comic device is an AD signature. The instant deflation of Michael's gesture — revealed before the scene even plays out — is crisp and economical. The joke works because Michael is presented as the competent one, yet he can't even pull off a basic surprise party setup.

jokes.text: Maeby says 'Would it kill her to let some vodka go bad?' about Lucille, then catches herself: 'Oh, no, vodka doesn't...'
jokes.setup: The earlier joke established that 'vodka goes bad once opened' is one of Lucille's invented myths.
jokes.punchline: Maeby has absorbed the myth so thoroughly she almost corrects herself mid-sentence — then catches that it's false. Callback to joke #4.
jokes.explanation: AD is famous for fast callbacks, and this one is tight — less than two minutes after the original joke. Maeby's near-correction shows how thoroughly Lucille's self-serving myths have infected even her grandchildren. The trailing 'Oh, no, vodka doesn't...' is a perfect half-beat that trusts the audience to complete the thought.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'You know, it's my birthday in a week and I bet you they don't even know. They forget every year. I should just move out.'
jokes.setup: The family is literally planning a surprise party for Maeby right now.
jokes.punchline: Maeby (who already knows about the party) dramatically plays the victim to herself — the audience holds both truths at once.
jokes.explanation: Functional irony — Maeby is performing victimhood she knows is false. Works but doesn't rise above competent because the joke is mostly structural irony without a strong punchline.

jokes.text: Buster tells Maeby about the surprise party immediately, saying 'They wouldn't forget your 16th birthday.'
jokes.setup: Maeby says they forget every year and she should move out
jokes.punchline: Buster instantly blows the surprise
jokes.explanation: Buster's eager-to-please naivety perfectly expressed. He literally cannot hold a secret for more than one sentence.

jokes.text: George Sr. reveals the family's ages: 'Our little girl's turning 14.' Lucille: '16, actually.' George: 'Well, I am off to buy the perfect present. Maybe she'd like a suit like this.' Lucille: 'That is her suit.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. doesn't know how old Maeby is turning, then immediately offers to buy her a gift — specifically the suit he's wearing.
jokes.punchline: He's already wearing her suit. The escalating parental incompetence: wrong age → thoughtless gift → wearing her clothes.
jokes.explanation: Three layered reveals in quick succession: the wrong age, the absurd gift suggestion, then the twist that the suit is actually Maeby's — meaning George Sr. has been wearing his granddaughter's suit this whole time. Good escalation structure.

jokes.text: George Sr. on Maeby's suit: 'They probably don't make it in a woman's though.' Lucille: 'They only make it in a woman's.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. has just been told the suit he's wearing belongs to Maeby.
jokes.punchline: His instinct is to assume it can't be a women's suit — but it's exclusively a women's suit. The specificity of 'They only make it in a woman's' is the deadpan kicker.
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy. The punchline is exactly five words and completely destroys George Sr.'s defensive rationalization. Lucille's delivery (as written) is admirably dry.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'Well, I'm off, my love. You see, sometimes shame can be fun.' — then instantly corrects to 'Secrets. / You said shame. / He said shame.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is leaving while making a quip, slips and says 'shame' instead of 'secrets.'
jokes.punchline: The Freudian slip is immediately caught and escalated: Lucille calls it out, Michael confirms it, then they speculate about what shameful secret he's referencing.
jokes.explanation: The Freudian slip gag works because the show has established that George Sr. has many secrets and shames. The rapid round-robin — George corrects himself, Lucille and Michael both call it out independently, then they start theorizing — is well-structured and shows the family's collective paranoia. Rewatch bonus because knowing George's many secrets makes the slip richer.

jokes.text: Gob reveals he's going to Iraq for a USO tour doing a 'Christian magic act' — dressing as Jesus and walking on water.
jokes.setup: Michael is worried about the prosecution watching the family; Gob casually reveals he's going to Iraq.
jokes.punchline: Gob's 'out of town act' is a Christian magic show where he impersonates Jesus performing miracles.
jokes.explanation: This is a high-craft joke because it's quintessentially Gob — his grandiosity, his magical ambition, and his complete tonal obliviousness combine into something absurdly specific. Dressing as Jesus for a USO tour in Iraq is a conceptually rich premise that earns its score. It also recalls multiple earlier season threads (Ann, the magic act, his water illusion). Character integration is near-perfect.

jokes.text: Gob says 'I do the wine into water...' Michael corrects 'It's water into wine.' Gob: 'Then I emptied out all your wine bottles and filled them with fake stuff for nothing.'
jokes.setup: Gob describes the miracles in his Jesus act, getting them backwards.
jokes.punchline: The correction reveals Gob has already sabotaged Michael's wine supply with fake liquid based on his misremembered miracle — a callback that pays off immediately.
jokes.explanation: This is a masterclass in AD's layered joke structure. The setup is Gob's theological error; the correction is the hinge; the punchline is that the error has already had real consequences (emptied wine bottles). Then Lindsay's 'Great, how am I supposed to have sex with my husband now?' immediately follows as a tag that reveals her marriage is also wine-dependent. The structure is near-perfect: three beats, each topping the last. Justifies an 8+ because the construction is genuinely impressive.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'Great, how am I supposed to have sex with my husband now?' — reacting to Gob emptying the wine bottles.
jokes.setup: Gob has replaced all the wine with fake liquid for his magic act.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's only concern is that she needs wine to have sex with Tobias — confirming both her alcoholism and the loveless marriage.
jokes.explanation: The line is sharp, economical, and does triple work: confirms Lindsay's drinking problem, lampshades the sexless marriage, and is a great button on joke #17. The phrasing 'my husband' rather than Tobias's name adds a slightly distant, formal quality that makes it funnier. High quotability.

jokes.text: Gob: 'You're just jealous that I am the son that the father has asked to do his work in the Holy Land.' Then: 'And he won't bump me up to business class.'
jokes.setup: Gob delivers a grandiose speech about being chosen by his father to do work in the 'Holy Land' (Iraq).
jokes.punchline: The divine mission deflates instantly into a petty complaint about his flight upgrade.
jokes.explanation: The gap between the grandiose (chosen by the father to do God's work in the Holy Land) and the petty (not bumped to business class) is the entire joke, and it's perfectly executed. The religious metaphor layered on the Iraq trip makes the business class complaint even more bathetic. Very Gob.

jokes.text: Tobias's 'audition' for the prosecutor: 'A flower in my garden, a mystery in my panties.' Wayne Jarvis: 'Again, this is not an audition for a CBS show.' Tobias: 'I was just looking for some feedback.' Jarvis: 'It was a little contrived.'
jokes.setup: Tobias believes he's auditioning for a TV show and performs a piece for the prosecutor.
jokes.punchline: His chosen monologue is 'A flower in my garden, a mystery in my panties' — bizarre, inappropriate, and definitively not TV audition material. Jarvis's 'It was a little contrived' is a perfect deadpan critique.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. 'A flower in my garden, a mystery in my panties' is a genuinely inspired absurdist line — it sounds like it could be poetry, it's vaguely sexual, it's completely context-inappropriate, and it's specific enough to be quotable. Jarvis treating it as merely 'a little contrived' rather than completely insane is the perfect deadpan response. The line has become one of Arrested Development's most quoted moments. Justifies 8+ across the board.

jokes.text: Tobias defends his family to Jarvis: people 'who wouldn't know good acting if it marched through the door and gave them headshots and decorative soaps.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is refusing to cooperate with the prosecution on principle.
jokes.punchline: His indignant defense of his family pivots into a bizarre acting-industry grievance — specifically that prosecutors don't understand 'good acting' as evidenced by their failure to recognize the headshots and decorative soaps gifted to casting agents.
jokes.explanation: 'Decorative soaps' is the key word that makes this joke work. Headshots are expected; the addition of decorative soaps as a standard actor gift to casting directors is the specific, absurd detail that elevates it. It sounds like something Tobias genuinely does and believes in.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'He's a classic scrapbooker. Right down to the woman's suit.' — as Tobias takes the scrapbooking flier.
jokes.setup: Tobias spots a flier for a scrapbooking class outside the prosecutor's office.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's 'He's a classic scrapbooker. Right down to the woman's suit.' links Tobias's new hobby to the earlier suit joke, while 'classic scrapbooker' implies this is a recognizable type.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the women's suit is a quick, efficient runner. The narrator's tone ('classic scrapbooker') is deadpan and implies social typing, which is a subtle additional layer. Sets up the scrapbooking sting that pays off later.

jokes.text: George Sr. says Buster 'took coma' and Lucille 'took rehab' as legal excuses. Michael: 'You're going to rehab again?' George Sr.: 'I'm bringing Lindsay. I'm worried about her.' Lucille: 'Did you get the corkscrew?'
jokes.setup: George Sr. explains Lucille is going to rehab for concern about Lindsay's drinking.
jokes.punchline: Lucille immediately asks about a corkscrew — confirming she's bringing alcohol to rehab and the 'concern for Lindsay' is a pretense.
jokes.explanation: The corkscrew punchline is tight and character-perfect for Lucille. Her concern for Lindsay's drinking is immediately exposed as hypocritical cover by the corkscrew question. The setup/reveal structure is clean.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'So there's going to be wine at the rehab, huh?' Lucille: 'Well, wine only turns into alcohol if you let it sit.'
jokes.setup: Lucille has confirmed she's bringing wine to rehab. George Sr. calls it out gently.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's invented factoid — wine only becomes alcohol if it sits — is a perfect mirror of Tobias's 'vodka goes bad' myth from earlier. Both are transparently false justifications for drinking immediately.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's strongest jokes and justifies its high score. The callback to the vodka myth (which is now confirmed as a pattern of Lucille inventing pseudo-science to justify drinking) is elegant. 'Wine only turns into alcohol if you let it sit' is a perfectly constructed absurd logic — it's internally coherent as a statement, just entirely false, and it makes Lucille's character both funnier and more pathetic. Michael's 'How do you come up with these?' is the ideal straight-man button. Economy: 9 words deliver the whole joke.

jokes.text: Michael: 'How do you come up with these?' — reacting to Lucille's wine-into-alcohol myth.
jokes.setup: Lucille delivers her 'wine only becomes alcohol if you let it sit' line.
jokes.punchline: Michael's genuine bewilderment, expressed as almost-admiring curiosity, is the perfect button. He's not angry — just staggered by her creativity.
jokes.explanation: The reaction beat earns its own entry because it reframes Lucille's absurdism as almost impressive — Michael treats her inventions as a skill rather than a problem. It's a subtle character moment that adds warmth to the comedy.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'This constant bickering is going to put me in a coma!' Michael: 'Nice try, Dad.'
jokes.setup: Buster is faking a coma to avoid testifying. George Sr. dramatically claims bickering will send him to a coma.
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately sees through it as a transparent attempt to copy Buster's legal excuse.
jokes.explanation: 'Nice try, Dad' is an excellent two-word punchline. The joke is about the Bluth family's casual fraud — George Sr. not only knows about Buster's fake coma, he's immediately trying to replicate it. Michael's weary recognition rather than shock says everything about this family.

jokes.text: Buster is serving as a 'live body' for medical students, a human canvas for beauticians, AND a set of teeth for at-risk youth dental trainees — all while 'in a coma.'
jokes.setup: Buster is faking a coma. The narrator reveals what this actually involves.
jokes.punchline: An escalating list of increasingly undignified and painful uses for his body: medical students practicing on him, beauticians using him for collagen injections, and dental students (named 'Turf' and 'Dogbone') practicing extractions.
jokes.explanation: The escalation structure here is well-constructed: each use of Buster's body is more humiliating than the last, culminating in dental students named 'Turf' and 'Dogbone' (the at-risk youth names are themselves a joke). 'It really was amazing for their self-esteem' is the darkly perfect narrator button. The joke earns its darkness because Buster brought this on himself through his own cowardice.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'That's okay, you put too much collagen in there. No, no, try it again.' — describing beauticians practicing on the comatose Buster.
jokes.setup: Buster is being used as a practice dummy for beauticians.
jokes.punchline: The instructor's patient, encouraging feedback ('try it again') directed at people injecting too much collagen into a supposedly comatose man is darkly comic in its normalcy.
jokes.explanation: The encouragement-while-doing-something-horrible structure ('that's okay, try again') is a good bit, though it's part of the larger escalation sequence rather than a standalone joke. The clinical normalcy of the instructor is the comedic engine.

jokes.text: Dental student named 'Dogbone' has his forearm on Buster's windpipe. Instructor: 'Oops, you've got your forearm right on his windpipe there, Dogbone.' Narrator: 'It really was amazing for their self-esteem.'
jokes.setup: Dental students are practicing on Buster. One is accidentally strangling him.
jokes.punchline: The instructor gently corrects 'Dogbone' for crushing Buster's windpipe, then the narrator observes it was 'amazing for their self-esteem' — completing the joke by prioritizing the students' feelings over Buster's survival.
jokes.explanation: The name 'Dogbone' (as a dental at-risk youth) is a small inspired detail. The narrator's pivot to 'self-esteem' as the metric while Buster is being accidentally asphyxiated is the darkest and funniest beat of the sequence. 'It really was amazing for their self-esteem' reads like a PSA about youth programs while describing something appalling.

jokes.text: Adelaide tells Buster: 'Take a single step to show me your love, Gary.' — then Buster, who has been on his back for weeks, actually tries to step and falls. Narrator: 'It was love that made Gary take that step. Of course, he hadn't used his legs in three weeks.'
jokes.setup: Adelaide is frustrated with Buster (Gary) and demands he prove his love by taking a step. She believes this is impossible.
jokes.punchline: Buster attempts it out of love, falls (inferred), and the narrator explains he hadn't used his legs in three weeks — meaning the fall is both inevitable and self-inflicted.
jokes.explanation: The structure is a classic falling-on-a-petard joke — Buster's love defeats his own con. 'Of course, he hadn't used his legs in three weeks' is the perfect narrator button, delivered with total detachment. The vegetable line — 'Oh, whoever said vegetables were good for your heart' — is also a nice bit as the setup to the fall.

jokes.text: Adelaide: 'Oh, whoever said vegetables were good for your heart.' — as Buster collapses trying to walk.
jokes.setup: Buster falls trying to take a step after weeks of lying in bed as a 'vegetable.'
jokes.punchline: 'Vegetables are good for your heart' is a health cliché; Adelaide is using 'vegetable' to mean Buster in a comatose state. The pun is dark and elegant.
jokes.explanation: A tight pun that works on two levels simultaneously — health advice and human-vegetable insult — while also referencing the heart literally breaking (she's heartbroken he was faking). Economy is excellent; the line does its work in ten words.

jokes.text: Wayne Jarvis reveals the two people who checked into Shady Pines under the names 'Lucille Bluth' and 'Lindsay Funke' are not Michael's relatives.
jokes.setup: Michael insisted his mother and sister are legitimately in rehab.
jokes.punchline: The prosecution has identified the decoys — the names were used but the actual people aren't the Bluths.
jokes.explanation: Functional comedy of Michael's incompetence as family manager — his 'legal excuses' are transparently fake. More plot than joke, but the reveal has comedic value in exposing Michael's confidence as misplaced.

jokes.text: Jarvis: 'Also, for someone in a coma, your brother Buster has made an impressive 15 phone calls to radio stations alternately requesting I Will Survive and...' — 'She's Out of My Life.'
jokes.setup: Buster is supposedly in a coma. The prosecution has been monitoring him.
jokes.punchline: He's been calling radio stations requesting songs — specifically the two-song combo of 'I Will Survive' and 'She's Out of My Life' perfectly captures his complicated feelings about Adelaide.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke and justifies a top score. The construction is perfect: Buster is supposed to be comatose; 15 phone calls to radio stations is already absurd; but the song selection is the masterstroke. 'I Will Survive' (I don't need you) alternating with 'She's Out of My Life' (I miss her desperately) perfectly maps Buster's emotional turmoil while also being hilarious as the specific activity that blew his cover. The two-beat payoff — pausing to get the second song title from his colleague — adds comic timing. This joke rewards knowledge of both songs. Genuinely exceptional structure.

jokes.text: Michael and Jarvis try to watch Gob on the news. The segment keeps being delayed by ads for insulin delivery and other commercials. Michael: 'I'm sure it's coming right up.' / 'I'm sure it's right after this series of ads.' Jarvis: 'Why don't we just go online?' Michael: 'Cool your Japanese jets.'
jokes.setup: A news segment about Gob in Iraq is supposedly playing, but they can't get to it.
jokes.punchline: The running frustration of cable news — endlessly delayed by commercials — is the joke; 'Cool your Japanese jets' is an oddly specific phrase that adds character to Michael.
jokes.explanation: Competent observational comedy about cable news, mildly elevated by 'Cool your Japanese jets' as a character tic. Not especially memorable but functional. The sequence serves more as structural delay before the payoff of seeing Gob on screen.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'And imagine the impact if that had come on right when we turned on the TV.' — after Gob's segment finally plays, way too late.
jokes.setup: The news segment about Gob finally airs after multiple commercial delays, making it an anticlimactic reveal.
jokes.punchline: The narrator explicitly points out that the comedic impact would have been better with better timing — a meta-joke about the scene's own failed timing.
jokes.explanation: This is a genuinely clever meta-joke that only works because the narrator has been a reliable, dry presence throughout. The narrator commenting on a scene's failed comic timing — essentially critiquing the show's own production — is self-aware without being smug. It retroactively makes the commercial-delay sequence funnier by acknowledging it was structurally unsatisfying. Justifies 8+ because it's an innovative use of the narrator device.

jokes.text: Buster: 'I'm not faking it, Michael. I really am this expressionless.'
jokes.setup: Michael tells Buster he can stop pretending to be in a coma since his radio requests were heard.
jokes.punchline: Buster insists his expressionlessness is genuine — implying his resting state is indistinguishable from a coma.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully economical joke that reveals something genuinely sad and funny about Buster's character — he's so repressed and passive that he naturally appears comatose. 'I really am this expressionless' as a defense is both tragic and hilarious. The delivery (as written) suggests complete sincerity, which is the joke.

jokes.text: Buster claims to speak Arabic. Michael: 'You speak Arabic?' Buster: 'La-ach-ha-tem.' Michael: 'What's that mean?' Buster: 'No, I was just clearing my throat, but I think it actually does mean laundry but like, a child's laundry. We don't really have a word for it.'
jokes.setup: Buster volunteers to help in Iraq, claiming to speak the language.
jokes.punchline: His 'Arabic' is just throat-clearing; then he doubles down by claiming his throat-clear happens to be the word for 'a child's laundry' — a concept so specific it requires a disclaimer that 'we don't really have a word for it' in English.
jokes.explanation: The joke escalates brilliantly. First beat: claiming to speak Arabic. Second: the 'word' is just throat-clearing. Third — and this is the standout beat — Buster hedges by saying it might mean 'laundry but like, a child's laundry. We don't really have a word for it.' The specificity of 'child's laundry' as a concept requiring its own word, combined with Buster's confidence in his own knowledge, is genuinely original and absurdist. Earns its 8+ because the third beat is unexpected and the logic of the explanation is delightfully broken.

jokes.text: George Michael considers Maeby's address book and decides 'I don't think Steve Holt has to come to this.' — but does invite Mickey Rourke.
jokes.setup: George Michael is going through Maeby's address book to invite friends to her party.
jokes.punchline: He selectively excludes Steve Holt (a rival) for self-interested reasons, but includes Mickey Rourke — a non-sequitur celebrity inclusion that implies Maeby knows Mickey Rourke.
jokes.explanation: Two-part joke with good structure. First, George Michael's jealousy-driven exclusion of Steve Holt is a character-specific callback to their rivalry. Then 'And yet he did invite Mickey Rourke' is the absurdist button — the narrator's 'and yet' makes it funnier by treating the Mickey Rourke invitation as a logical contrast. Maeby being the kind of teenager who has Mickey Rourke in her address book is a perfect character detail.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'The scrapbooking sting had helped the D.A. gather evidence against people as diverse as Ken Lay... Oliver North, but ironically not Martha Stewart.'
jokes.setup: The D.A.'s scrapbooking sting is revealed as a broader operation.
jokes.punchline: The sting caught Ken Lay and Oliver North but not Martha Stewart — whose actual criminal conviction was famously related to paper records and who is associated with crafts (including scrapbooking), making her immunity to a scrapbooking sting the ironic exception.
jokes.explanation: The Martha Stewart punchline is the standout. Her omission is 'ironic' — she's the one person you'd expect to be ensnared by a craft-supply sting, yet she escaped. The joke rewards knowledge of her actual crimes (insider trading, paper records) and her crafting brand simultaneously. Ken Lay and Oliver North as comparators are good escalating company before the Stewart twist. Justifies 8+ for the multi-layered topical knowledge required to fully appreciate it.

jokes.text: D.A. instructor: 'And remember, photocopies are not admissible as memories.'
jokes.setup: The scrapbooking class instructor is wrapping up, reminding participants to use originals.
jokes.punchline: 'Photocopies are not admissible as memories' — a legal term ('admissible') applied to the concept of authentic personal recollection vs. copies in the context of a craft class that is secretly a criminal sting.
jokes.explanation: A densely packed line: 'admissible' is both a legal term and used correctly here (only originals are admissible as evidence), while 'memories' replaces 'evidence.' The dual meaning works on both the scrapbooking (memories are authentic, photocopies aren't) and legal (evidence must be originals) levels simultaneously. Economical and quotable.

jokes.text: The scrapbooking class turns out to be a D.A. sting — the pre-made template pages include 'Foreign Bank Statements' and 'Shh! Family Secrets' alongside 'My Favorite Birthday.'
jokes.setup: Tobias thinks he's attending a legitimate scrapbooking class to make Maeby's birthday gift.
jokes.punchline: The ready-made pages reveal this is a targeted sting operation — the bait pages specifically designed to extract criminal evidence are listed alongside innocent birthday categories.
jokes.explanation: The joke is the incongruity of 'Foreign Bank Statements' and 'Shh! Family Secrets' as scrapbook page categories sitting alongside normal memory pages. The 'Shh!' on 'Family Secrets' is a particularly funny detail — the D.A. added a playful shushing emoji-equivalent to a criminal evidence trap. High craft for the conceptual specificity.

jokes.text: Lindsay and Lucille at the spa: 'Four saunas, three steam baths, Olympic sized pool and you're telling me there's no alcohol?' Lucille: 'What the hell are we supposed to do for two days.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay and Lucille are at a luxury spa, supposedly for 'rehab.'
jokes.punchline: The extensive luxury amenities are irrelevant; the single missing element (alcohol) renders everything else worthless. Their shared helplessness without alcohol is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Competent character comedy about Lucille and Lindsay's shared alcoholism. The inventory of spa luxuries before the complaint is well-structured. Not especially original but well-executed.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'I could take the top off this grape juice and maybe by morning...' Lucille: 'Oh, for God's sake, Lindsay, grow up. You're 40 years old.' Lindsay: 'Thirty-five.'
jokes.setup: They have no alcohol; Lindsay suggests waiting for grape juice to ferment overnight.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's 'grow up, you're 40' is followed by Lindsay's '35' correction — Lucille doesn't know her own daughter's age (echoing George Sr. not knowing Maeby's age), and Lindsay is defensive about the number.
jokes.explanation: The grape juice fermentation plan is funny in itself, but Lucille's wrong age and Lindsay's correction is the sharper beat — and it mirrors the earlier Maeby age mistake, establishing Bluth age-blindness as a running family trait. The Lindsay-Lucille dynamic (Lucille condescending, Lindsay self-correcting) is well-deployed.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'Well, maybe this will be nice. We can talk... and connect a little.' — then immediately: 'I must have a Xanax.' Lindsay: 'I'll go score some weed from the staff.'
jokes.setup: Lucille proposes genuine mother-daughter bonding at the spa.
jokes.punchline: The suggestion of emotional connection lasts approximately one second before both immediately seek drugs.
jokes.explanation: The brevity of the 'connection' suggestion before both characters immediately reach for substances is the entire joke. The '...' in 'talk... and connect a little' suggests even the proposal is painful. Then Lucille goes for prescription drugs while Lindsay goes for recreational weed — even their drug choices are character-specific.

jokes.text: The taxi driver arrives and 'went into a coma as soon as they pulled up to the hospital.'
jokes.setup: The episode has been running the 'fake coma' plot. A new character — the cab driver — now falls into an apparent coma at the hospital.
jokes.punchline: Adelaide receives a new coma patient (the cab driver) just as Buster is leaving — suggesting coma patients are cyclically replacing each other in her emotional life, and/or the driver saw an opportunity.
jokes.explanation: The suggestion that Adelaide attracts/creates coma patients — or that word has spread among taxi drivers about the romantic opportunities at this hospital — is an absurdist extension of the coma bit. 'What a brave man' from Adelaide confirms she's already emotionally moved by this stranger.

jokes.text: Buster: 'When I miss your lips, I'll put a fig in my mouth and think of you. They have figs over there, right?'
jokes.setup: Buster is saying a romantic goodbye to Adelaide
jokes.punchline: The fig metaphor is bizarre but delivered with total sincerity, then immediately undercut by the practical question of fig availability in Iraq
jokes.explanation: Outstanding Buster line. The fig metaphor is both oddly poetic and deeply weird (why a fig?), and then the practical follow-up question destroys any romantic air while revealing Buster takes the plan completely seriously. Could only be Buster. One of the episode's most quotable moments.

jokes.text: Narrator describes arriving in Iraq, then: 'He said he's taking surface streets to LAX because the freeway's backed up all the way to Knott's Berry Farm.' — the cab driver is navigating Los Angeles while purportedly in Baghdad.
jokes.setup: Michael and Buster have just arrived in 'Iraq' via rickshaw.
jokes.punchline: The cab driver's directions reference LAX and Knott's Berry Farm — they're either still in Los Angeles, or the Iraq/LA confusion is the joke (matching the show's tendency to film LA as any location).
jokes.explanation: The Knott's Berry Farm reference is a specific, local comedy choice that works by being hyper-specific about a mundane LA landmark. The disconnect (Iraq setting, LA directions) is funny as absurdism and also as a low-key meta-joke about TV production.

jokes.text: Buster, instead of a cab, 'hired them the only rickshaw in Baghdad.'
jokes.setup: Buster's Arabic was supposed to get them transportation in Baghdad.
jokes.punchline: His language skills got them the 'only rickshaw in Baghdad' — both absurdly impractical and somehow on-brand for Buster's incompetence.
jokes.explanation: A visual gag/payoff for the Arabic joke. 'The only rickshaw in Baghdad' is a precise and funny image — it implies Buster's linguistic incompetence is so profound it somehow summoned the most anachronistic vehicle available. Good payoff for the earlier setup.

jokes.text: Gob: 'So you ready for Operation Hot Brother?' Michael: 'I'm not ready to call it that.'
jokes.setup: They're heading to the Iraqi prison to rescue Gob.
jokes.punchline: Gob has given the operation a self-aggrandizing name ('Hot Brother') that Michael flatly refuses to use.
jokes.explanation: A tight two-line exchange that perfectly captures the Gob/Michael dynamic: Gob's vanity, Michael's exasperation. 'I'm not ready to call it that' is understated and perfect — he's not rejecting it outright, just not ready, as if the name might grow on him.

jokes.text: The Iraqi prison guards are being trained by Americans — and they're practicing walking prisoners through a metal detector while forgetting to actually turn it on. 'The what? / The metal detector. / That's okay. Try again.'
jokes.setup: The prison is described as one 'the Americans were training Iraqis to run.'
jokes.punchline: The training involves the instructor patiently coaching guards through the most basic security procedure — and getting it wrong — using the exact same 'That's okay, try again' phrasing from the beautician/dental scenes.
jokes.explanation: The 'That's okay, try again' motif is now a running gag across the episode — appearing in the hospital training scenes, and now again in the prison context. The dark satirical edge (incompetent prison staff being trained through cheerful encouragement) aligns the comedy with real-world Abu Ghraib-era critique, which the show is clearly gesturing at. The callback to the same instructor phrasing is structurally satisfying.

jokes.text: Gob, found in prison, greets Michael with: 'Oh, I missed you, Michael! All right. Or should I say robot. What, 'cause I'm not crying? Maybe you don't have enough RAM to understand this, Michael, but there's such a thing as brotherly love.'
jokes.setup: Michael arrives to rescue Gob from an Iraqi prison. Gob is emotional.
jokes.punchline: Gob is crying; Michael isn't. Gob attacks Michael for his emotional restraint by calling him a robot lacking sufficient RAM to understand love — a bizarre tech metaphor delivered with full sincerity.
jokes.explanation: The RAM line is the highlight — Gob using computing metaphors to talk about emotional capacity is perfectly absurd. The reversal (typically Michael is the emotionally functional one while Gob is the needy disaster) adds some irony. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Gob explains his act: 'And that is why Jesus was often referred to as the King of Kings.' Guard: 'Queens.' Gob: 'The King of Queens.'
jokes.setup: Gob is explaining his Jesus magic act to the prison. He says Jesus was 'King of Kings.'
jokes.punchline: The guard corrects 'Queens' (as in the CBS show 'King of Queens') and Gob immediately integrates it — saying 'The King of Queens' — treating a biblical title correction as a TV show reference.
jokes.explanation: The guard's 'Queens' correction is itself ambiguous (is he correcting the theology or referencing the show?) and Gob's seamless pivot to 'The King of Queens' is fast and clean. The joke relies on the audience holding both meanings simultaneously. Good economy.

jokes.text: Gob performs 'The Burning Bush' illusion — with the crowd chanting 'Burn Bush, burn Bush' — which gets mistaken for an anti-American political demonstration.
jokes.setup: Gob is doing a magic trick called 'The Burning Bush' (a biblical reference) for an Iraqi crowd.
jokes.punchline: The crowd's 'Burn Bush!' chants are read as anti-Bush (the president) political protest by security forces who come to suppress it.
jokes.explanation: This is a sharp political/absurdist joke that works on multiple levels. The biblical 'Burning Bush' as an illusion name, the phonetic ambiguity between 'burn bush' (fire effect) and 'Burn Bush' (anti-presidential protest), and the consequence (military suppression) form a satisfying comic chain. It's topical for its era and still funny as a demonstration of how easily Gob creates catastrophes through obliviousness. Justifies 8+ for the layered construction and political bite.

jokes.text: During the 'Burn Bush' suppression scene, the 'That's okay, try again' motif appears again — now applied to soldiers trying to deploy crowd control.
jokes.setup: The 'That's okay, try again' patient-instructor refrain has appeared in hospital beauty/dental training and prison guard training.
jokes.punchline: Now it appears again in a military crowd control context — the incompetent encouragement motif is universal and cuts across all institutions.
jokes.explanation: The running gag's third appearance starts to feel thematic — every institution (medical, security, military) uses the same inadequate training methodology. The institutional critique is doing more work by this point, and the cumulative effect is stronger than any single iteration.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Well, Dad... I mean, he had said if I happened to be in the neighborhood of Sadr City or Momoon, would I mind torching a couple model homes.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks if Gob is really in Iraq doing anything for their father
jokes.punchline: Gob casually reveals his father asked him to burn down model homes as if it were a neighborly errand — 'if I happened to be in the neighborhood'
jokes.explanation: The casualness of Gob's confession is perfect — 'if I happened to be in the neighborhood' makes arson in Sadr City sound like picking up milk. Perfectly captures both Gob's obliviousness and George Sr.'s criminal exploitation of his children.

jokes.text: Gob admits George Sr. asked him to 'torch a couple model homes' in Sadr City — 'Dad's just trying to keep information out of the hands of the Iraqis.' Michael: 'I have a feeling he was trying to keep it out of the hands of the federal marshals.'
jokes.setup: Michael suspected George Sr. was behind Gob's Iraq trip for ulterior motives.
jokes.punchline: Gob reveals the mission (torch model homes) and defends it with George Sr.'s cover story. Michael immediately sees through the transparent lie.
jokes.explanation: The joke is the contrast between Gob's credulity (accepting a transparently false explanation) and Michael's instant clarity. Functional but not especially inventive — the show has done the 'Gob is a useful idiot for George Sr.' beat many times.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Oh shoot, I forgot to say good-bye to Yusef.' — then they're immediately released. Narrator: 'That was a lot easier than I expected. Almost too easy.'
jokes.setup: Michael said getting Gob out of an enemy combatant prison 'might not be that easy.'
jokes.punchline: Gob's excuse to go back in (say goodbye to Yusef) somehow results in instant release. The narrator's 'Almost too easy' — which will pay off when the CIA reveal confirms it WAS too easy on purpose.
jokes.explanation: Functions as foreshadowing more than standalone comedy. The narrator's 'Almost too easy' is a good suspense beat with a knowing wink. On rewatch, the CIA reveal makes the entire prison sequence retroactively funnier.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Oh, shoot, I forgot to say good-bye to Yusef.' / [They immediately walk out of prison] / Narrator: 'That was a lot easier than I expected. Almost too easy.'
jokes.setup: Michael warns Gob that as an enemy combatant, leaving prison 'won't be that easy'
jokes.punchline: Gob just... walks out to say goodbye to someone, and they all leave
jokes.explanation: The 'Almost too easy' narrator capper is doing the show's signature self-aware work — flagging that something is wrong with this picture, which is revealed later (it was a CIA setup). The rewatch bonus is significant because the joke retroactively changes meaning.

jokes.text: Iraqi cab driver: 'I'm sorry it took so long, but the Cheney Expressway was backed up all the way to Halliburton Drive.'
jokes.setup: Characters complaining about traffic delays is established with the earlier LAX/Knott's Berry Farm line.
jokes.punchline: In Iraq, the street names are 'Cheney Expressway' and 'Halliburton Drive' — a sharp satirical jab at war profiteering repackaged as a traffic complaint.
jokes.explanation: The political satire is sharp and specific. Using Cheney and Halliburton as Baghdad street names makes a pointed comment about the Iraq War's profiteers while working as a deadpan traffic joke. The callback structure (parallel traffic complaints) gives it extra structure. Quotable as a period political joke.

jokes.text: The Saddam Hussein look-alike wants his photo taken constantly — 'Take my picture! Take my picture!'
jokes.setup: Michael finds someone the U.S. government 'had been able to depose' in Iraq.
jokes.punchline: The 'deposed' person is a Saddam Hussein look-alike, and the word 'depose' has both the legal meaning (give testimony) and the political meaning (remove from power) — plus the look-alike is desperately seeking attention, not hiding.
jokes.explanation: The double meaning of 'depose' is the structural hook — clever wordplay that works because the show has been in deposition-land all episode. The look-alike's eagerness to be photographed is a good character bit.

jokes.text: The Saddam look-alike asks if Michael is there to 'fix the air-conditioning' — implying the Bluths are known in Iraq only as HVAC contractors.
jokes.setup: The Bluth Company built model homes in Iraq. The look-alikes know the name Bluth.
jokes.punchline: Their reputation in Iraq is strictly as air-conditioning repairers — their involvement in a major geopolitical situation reduced to a mundane service call.
jokes.explanation: The bathos of the Bluth Company's Iraq legacy being 'air-conditioning' is funny and thematically resonant — all this geopolitical intrigue and the Bluths are remembered for home comfort systems.

jokes.text: Multiple Saddam look-alikes debate the quality of each other's impressions. 'My Saddam would show his teeth.' / 'Yes, to chew the scenery.'
jokes.setup: Michael discovers a house full of Saddam Hussein look-alikes all living together.
jokes.punchline: 'Chew the scenery' is a theatrical idiom for overacting — applied literally to Saddam showing his teeth, plus as a critique of the other look-alike's performance. Layered theatrical/political joke.
jokes.explanation: 'Chew the scenery' as a pun works on multiple levels — literal (teeth), theatrical (overacting critique), and political (Saddam's performative public persona). The look-alikes arguing about the quality of their Saddam impressions is inherently absurd and the pun sharpens it.

jokes.text: One look-alike: 'Yes, and we wanted to keep the house for just one more season.' Another: 'I have a baby on the way.'
jokes.setup: Michael notes that if Saddam is convicted, the look-alikes are 'out of a job.'
jokes.punchline: The look-alikes respond with ordinary worker anxieties — wanting to keep the house 'one more season' (like a TV show being cancelled) and paternity concerns. Their genocidal employer's downfall = their unemployment.
jokes.explanation: 'One more season' also reads as meta-commentary — Arrested Development was itself fighting for renewal at this point, adding a self-referential layer to the look-alike's plea. Whether intentional or not, it resonates. The banality of their concerns (housing, babies) against the backdrop of Saddam's trial is dark comedy done well.

jokes.text: Look-alike: 'I do a better Saddam than this guy.' / Other look-alike: 'My Saddam would show his teeth.' / 'Yes, to chew the scenery.'
jokes.setup: Look-alikes critique each other's Saddam impressions
jokes.punchline: 'To chew the scenery' — a theatrical acting term applied literally, meaning both 'overact' and 'Saddam would gnaw on set decoration'
jokes.explanation: Tight theatrical wordplay — 'chew the scenery' applied to the acting quality of a dictator look-alike is a perfectly layered pun. Works on multiple levels without being labored.

jokes.text: George Michael's 'surprise' party for Maeby has zero guests. Maeby: 'Yeah, that's what I figured.' George Michael: 'I really sorry they didn't come. I don't know what happened.' Maeby: 'I do. They found out I was 16. And a lot of us just didn't want to drive down to Orange County.'
jokes.setup: George Michael invited Maeby's friends to a Sweet 16 party; nobody came.
jokes.punchline: Maeby explains the dual reasons: her real age revealed (implying her friends thought she was older) and the Orange County location being a social deterrent.
jokes.explanation: Maeby's life as a fake adult is a major plot thread, and the party's failure for age-revelation reasons is a good ironic payoff. 'A lot of us just didn't want to drive down to Orange County' is a funny regional slight delivered with perfect casualness.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'This cost me my job, George Michael. I was a studio executive. I made movies.'
jokes.setup: The party invitation revealed Maeby was 16, which apparently exposed her to her film industry colleagues.
jokes.punchline: Maeby having been a legitimate studio executive — and losing that job because a birthday party invitation revealed her real age — is a perfect encapsulation of her entire character arc.
jokes.explanation: 'I was a studio executive. I made movies.' delivered as an indignant grievance — by a 16-year-old — is a good character beat. The specificity of the complaint (she lost a real job) makes it funnier than generic 'I'm mature for my age' comedy.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'You know what, I'm a kid. I'm going to start acting like one.' George Michael: 'What, you're going to drink?'
jokes.setup: Maeby declares she's going to embrace being a teenager.
jokes.punchline: George Michael's interpretation of 'acting like a kid' is drinking — which is itself a reversal (kids drink to seem older; Maeby would be drinking to embrace youth).
jokes.explanation: The confusion about what 'acting like a kid' means — in a family where alcohol has been a constant — is a tight reversal. George Michael's sincere question ('What, you're going to drink?') is the character-specific punchline.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'But they didn't know it was the fake wine from Gob's magic show.' — as George Michael and Maeby happily drink.
jokes.setup: Gob had replaced all wine with fake liquid for his Jesus magic act.
jokes.punchline: The fake wine callback now pays off — George Michael and Maeby think they're drinking, but they're drinking prop liquid. Sets up potential future comedy.
jokes.explanation: A well-structured callback payoff. The wine was replaced early; now it pays off in an entirely different storyline. The fact that they're drinking happily ('It's good!') while consuming fake prop liquid is a solid ironic beat. Also sets up the George Michael/Maeby escalation later.

jokes.text: Michael finds a nuclear warhead. CIA agent: 'Looks like a mid-range nuclear warhead. Has a range capacity of 4,000 to 5,000 nautical miles.' Michael: 'You've got a pretty sophisticated knowledge of this technology.' CIA agent: 'My God, it makes me want to have an ice-cream cone. Doesn't this make you want to have an ice-cream cone?'
jokes.setup: Someone with expert nuclear weapons knowledge is speaking clinically about a warhead.
jokes.punchline: The person with sophisticated WMD knowledge pivots to wanting an ice cream cone — a completely non-sequitur desire that reveals this is Gob in disguise.
jokes.explanation: The pivot from clinical weapons expertise to ice cream desire is perfectly absurdist, and the CIA agent's insistence on making others share the ice cream craving ('Doesn't this make you want to have an ice-cream cone?') deepens it. The WMD-to-ice-cream leap is specifically funny because there's no logic bridge — it's just pure Gob wanting something. 'Ice cream sandwiches' as the correction/clarification is an excellent button. Justifies 8+ for originality and structure.

jokes.text: CIA agent: 'You'll have plenty of time for ice cream in prison.' Other agent: 'Ice cream sandwiches.' CIA agent: '...CIA undercover.'
jokes.setup: The CIA agent threatens Michael with prison; his partner corrects 'ice cream' to 'ice cream sandwiches.'
jokes.punchline: After the totally-off-topic correction, the agent says 'CIA undercover' — as if the ice cream sandwich correction was the reveal, not the agent's cover identity.
jokes.explanation: Good button on the ice cream joke. The CIA reveal lands well because it confirms what the ice cream suggestion implied — the agent is actually familiar (Gob-adjacent). The 'ice cream sandwiches' correction happening before the reveal of being CIA is a nice tonal subversion.

jokes.text: CIA agent explains they let Gob out of prison to follow him: 'Get in the cell, stupid.' / 'You... you're the one who didn't even shut the door.' — showing the 'escape' was staged.
jokes.setup: The CIA claims they engineered Gob's escape so he would lead them to whatever George Sr. was hiding.
jokes.punchline: The flashback shows the 'escape' involved guards arguing about who forgot to close the door — the 'sophisticated intelligence operation' is revealed to be slapstick incompetence.
jokes.explanation: The payoff for the 'almost too easy' foreshadowing. The CIA's intelligence operation reduced to 'you didn't shut the door' bickering is a good deflation of institutional competence, consistent with the episode's 'That's okay, try again' motif.

jokes.text: The CIA staged an 'honor marathon' to get Gob to run — showing Gob running through the prison yard. Narrator: 'They thought he was sure to take advantage of.'
jokes.setup: The CIA is explaining all the elaborate traps they set to engineer Gob's escape.
jokes.punchline: An 'honor marathon' — the CIA knew Gob would be unable to resist any competitive event, especially one where he can feel heroic.
jokes.explanation: The CIA's understanding of Gob's vanity is funny — they know exactly how to manipulate him. 'Honor marathon' as a CIA operation is absurdist and character-specific.

jokes.text: The WMD turns out to be a 'Homefill' — the same company that makes fake props for model homes. 'It's the company that makes the fake stuff we fill the model homes with.'
jokes.setup: Michael has found what looks like a nuclear warhead in his father's model home in Iraq.
jokes.punchline: The 'WMD' is actually a display prop from the Bluth Company's model home supplier — the entire WMD plotline deflates into a furniture catalog item.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes and earns its high score. The WMD-as-Homefill-prop is a devastating satirical move: it literalizes the 'WMDs were fake' critique of the Iraq War while simultaneously resolving the episode's mystery plot and connecting to the Bluth Company's mundane home-furnishing business. Three separate threads (WMDs, model homes, home decor) converge in one reveal. The structural elegance is exceptional. It also implies George Sr.'s model homes were used by the CIA to bug, not for WMDs — making the entire 'treason' charge absurdly banal. Fully justifies 8+ marks.

jokes.text: CIA phone call: 'Did we bug a house in Mamoon? Yeah. We got some guy named George Bluth to build houses down there so we could wire them.'
jokes.setup: The episode has built up George Sr.'s treason charges as a serious legal threat. Michael believed his father might genuinely be guilty.
jokes.punchline: The CIA casually confirms George Sr. was an unwitting patsy — they used his construction company as a surveillance front. He was innocent all along, but through mundane bureaucratic accident rather than virtue.
jokes.explanation: The reveal that George Sr.'s treasonous home-building was actually a CIA operation he didn't know about is satisfying as plot resolution and funny as character comedy — he's been insisting he was a patsy all along, and he was right, but it took CIA confirmation to believe him.

jokes.text: Buster tells Michael: 'You might not even be related to us... 'Cause Gangee once said to me, your mom might not have ever even been pregnant with you.'
jokes.setup: Buster is trying to comfort Michael by suggesting he's not really part of this dysfunctional family.
jokes.punchline: His comfort takes the form of suggesting Michael might not be biologically related — and his source is a questionable family story from 'Gangee.'
jokes.explanation: The Bluth family's convoluted parentage questions are a recurring theme. Buster's attempt at comfort — 'you might not be one of us, which is good news' — is both sweet and devastating. Michael's response ('Okay, now you're just saying that to make me feel better') accepting the logic of the insult as kindness is the best beat.

jokes.text: Buster: 'Okay, now you're just saying that to make me feel better.' / Michael: 'No, I'm... I wouldn't.' / Buster: 'Everyone still loves you. Some more than others.'
jokes.setup: Michael reveals Buster may not be related to them — as a consolation
jokes.punchline: Buster interprets 'you might not be our family' as a kindness — he's so damaged by his family that non-membership sounds like a gift
jokes.explanation: Deeply dark character comedy — Buster's instant positive interpretation of 'you're not our family' reveals how profoundly the Bluths have damaged him. 'Some more than others' is a brilliant qualifier that doesn't specify who loves him most (Lucille's disturbing attachment). One of the episode's most emotionally layered jokes.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Okay, now you're just saying that to make me feel better.' Buster: 'No, I... I wouldn't.' — Michael treating 'you might not be our relative' as a comforting fib.
jokes.setup: Buster has just told Michael he might not be biologically related to the Bluths.
jokes.punchline: Michael assumes Buster is lying to be kind (as you would if someone said something nice). The reversal: the 'nice' thing Buster could say is questioning Michael's legitimacy.
jokes.explanation: The punchline is that Michael has accepted 'not being a Bluth' as such a desirable condition that being told he might not be one is genuinely heartwarming — and he catches Buster's denial ('I wouldn't') as the lie of a good friend. The logic is perfectly inverted and character-specific to both men.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'And perhaps it was the fake wine, but that was the night George Michael and Maeby went to second base.'
jokes.setup: George Michael and Maeby have been drinking what they think is wine (actually Gob's fake prop liquid).
jokes.punchline: The narrator drops the bombshell — they went to second base — with 'perhaps it was the fake wine' as the most casual possible qualifier for a deeply disturbing cousins-getting-physical revelation.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's deadpan delivery of this revelation — 'perhaps it was the fake wine' — is a signature AD move. The word 'perhaps' does enormous work: it's the narrator being coy about something the show has been building toward for multiple seasons. The triple callback (fake wine → drinking scene → second base) pays off three setups simultaneously. The dark subject matter (cousins) handled through breezy narrator understatement is tonally perfect for the show. High impact because the room would audibly react.

jokes.text: CIA Agent: 'We call them "unintentional operations victims" but yes.' — re: George Sr. being a patsy
jokes.setup: Michael asks if his father was really just a patsy
jokes.punchline: The CIA's euphemism for 'patsy' is 'unintentional operations victims' — bureaucratic language that acknowledges the harm without admitting wrongdoing
jokes.explanation: Sharp institutional satire — the CIA's need to bureaucratize even casual confession is very well-observed. 'Unintentional operations victims' is a great coinage.

jokes.text: CIA agent: 'I'm afraid I'm going to have to take you guys out.' Michael: 'Take us out? Where?' Gob: 'I could really go for some Italian.' Buster: 'There is an Olive Garden in Mamoon.' Michael: 'I think he means to shoot you in the head.'
jokes.setup: CIA agent threatens to 'take them out' — a clear euphemism for killing them.
jokes.punchline: Gob and Buster interpret 'take you out' as a restaurant invitation. Michael has to clarify what the CIA agent means.
jokes.explanation: The 'take you out' misunderstanding is a familiar trope, but it's elevated by specificity: Gob wanting Italian and Buster knowing there's an Olive Garden in Mamoon (implying he's already scoped it out). Michael's 'I think he means to shoot you in the head' is a beautifully dry clarification. 'Shoot you in the head' rather than 'kill you' is the extra word-level craft.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Well, don't be mad, but I have been sending the pictures from my cell phone to my friend.' CIA agent panics. Michael: 'Well, who knows who she sent them to?'
jokes.setup: The CIA is about to eliminate the Bluth brothers as security threats.
jokes.punchline: Gob has been sending cell phone photos throughout the episode — and the CIA's panic confirms the photos are leverage. The ambiguity of 'who knows who she sent them to' (Michael implying the friend shared them) is the threat.
jokes.explanation: Gob's oblivious photo-taking throughout the episode pays off as an accidental spy move. Michael's quick pivot to leverage is smart. The CIA's over-the-top concern suggests the photos are more damning than we know, which is left pleasingly ambiguous.

jokes.text: Buster: 'Looks like you are a hero, Buster.' — addressing himself. Michael: 'We both are.' Buster: 'Well...'
jokes.setup: Gob's photos have saved them from the CIA; Michael credits both himself and Buster as heroes.
jokes.punchline: Buster's 'Well...' — a barely suppressed disagreement with including Michael in the heroism — is a perfect character beat of oblivious self-congratulation.
jokes.explanation: Buster narrating his own heroism to himself ('Looks like you are a hero, Buster') is a good character moment — he's been performing bravery for Adelaide all episode, and here he genuinely claims it for himself, then reluctantly has to share it. The 'Well...' as punchline is economical.

jokes.text: Their reward demand from the CIA: 'We're going to need some business class tickets home. And direct flights. None of this stopping in Phoenix bull[bleep].'
jokes.setup: The CIA asks what they can do for the Bluths in exchange for their silence.
jokes.punchline: After the episode's entire geopolitical stakes (WMDs, treason charges, CIA operations), their demand is business class tickets and no layovers in Phoenix.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes and earns its high score. The business class demand as the reward for helping the CIA resolve a geopolitical crisis is perfectly bathetic. 'None of this stopping in Phoenix bull[bleep]' is the quotable kicker — the Phoenix slight is so specific and petty it becomes funny in its own right. Callback structure is elegant: the episode opened with Gob wanting business class; it closes with him extracting it from the CIA. The circularity is satisfying.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'And so, with Operation Hot Brother a success...' — finally calling it by Gob's name.
jokes.setup: Michael refused to call the rescue mission 'Operation Hot Brother' at the start.
jokes.punchline: The narrator now uses the name unironically, validating Gob's branding in retrospect.
jokes.explanation: A clean callback button. The narrator adopting 'Operation Hot Brother' is funny because it acknowledges Gob won — his name for the mission has been officially accepted by the episode's framing device.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'And so, with Operation Hot Brother a success...'
jokes.setup: Michael refused to use that name earlier
jokes.punchline: The narrator officially uses 'Operation Hot Brother' in the after-action summary, overriding Michael's objection
jokes.explanation: The narrator retroactively validating Operation Hot Brother — the name Michael rejected — is a perfect structural callback. The narrator becoming a character who takes sides is very AD.

jokes.text: Cab driver: 'Take Condoleezza Rice Lane. It's quicker.' Then: 'Mission accomplished.'
jokes.setup: The Bluths are leaving Iraq successfully.
jokes.punchline: 'Mission accomplished' — a direct reference to the notorious Bush banner — used as a literal sign-off after the episode's Iraq adventure, spoken by an Iraqi cab driver.
jokes.explanation: 'Mission accomplished' from an Iraqi cab driver is a knowing political punchline that works because the entire episode has been a comedic treatment of Iraq War politics. 'Condoleezza Rice Lane' continues the street-name gag. The double political reference in one line is efficient.

jokes.text: 'On the Next Arrested Development' tag: Tobias gives Maeby a scrapbook with 'a picture of your mother pregnant' and 'a whole series of your head starting to crown.'
jokes.setup: Tobias was tasked with making Maeby a gift via the scrapbooking class.
jokes.punchline: His gift includes birth photography documentation ('head starting to crown') — presented as an appropriate and loving birthday gift.
jokes.explanation: The 'head starting to crown' detail is specifically gross in a way that fits the show's boundary-pushing sensibility. Tobias presenting birth photos as a loving gift is perfect Tobias — tone-deaf, enthusiastic, and inappropriate. The tag format (quick, punchy) suits the material well.

jokes.text: 'Look at you -- so full of wonder and shock. You still do it. And so does he! Boy, you guys really are related.' — as Tobias reacts to seeing the same expressions on Maeby and someone else.
jokes.setup: Tobias is sharing birth photos with Maeby, commenting on family resemblance.
jokes.punchline: Tobias's observation that their shared 'wonder and shock' expressions confirm they're related — but Maeby's parentage has been a running mystery/joke, so this confirmation is itself comedically loaded.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the dramatic irony — Tobias cheerfully confirming family resemblance in a family where parentage is chronically uncertain. Functions better in the context of the show's larger Maeby storyline.

jokes.text: 'On the Next': Michael and Gob encounter the 'real Saddam.' 'You've got the wrong guy. The real Saddam has a scar on his head. I'm No Scar. Dot com.'
jokes.setup: The episode has featured multiple Saddam Hussein look-alikes; one with 'no scar' was noted as a problem earlier.
jokes.punchline: 'I'm No Scar. Dot com.' — the Saddam look-alike has a personal brand ('No Scar') and a website, suggesting he's a professional Saddam impersonator with internet marketing.
jokes.explanation: Potentially the most quotable joke of the episode. 'I'm No Scar. Dot com.' as a personal brand for a Saddam look-alike is a genuinely inspired absurdist concept. The 'dot com' suffix transforms a distinguishing physical characteristic into a professional identity and URL. It's economical (four words do all the work), original (nothing quite like it in sitcom history), and earns its setup by turning a throwaway observation about the scar into a full character bit. High justification for 8+ across originality and economy.

jokes.text: 'Boy, you guys really are related.' — Tobias's closing observation
jokes.setup: Tobias notes George Michael and Maeby share Maeby's birth-photo expression
jokes.punchline: 'You guys really are related' — meant as an innocent observation about family resemblance, lands as confirmation of the disturbing romantic subplot
jokes.explanation: The episode's single best joke. Tobias's innocent affirmation of family resemblance — 'you guys really are related' — is terrible and perfect as a closer. It's meant as sweetness, lands as horror. It also ironically echoes Michael's claim to Buster that he MIGHT not be related, while here Tobias confirms the two people who shouldn't be related definitely are. The economy is extraordinary: five words do enormous comedic and thematic work. Classic AD final-beat construction.

tmdb_overview: Gob goes to Iraq to perform his Christian magic act on the USO tour and ends up incarcerated. Michael learns George, Sr. sent Gob to Iraq to burn down the model home he built there, so Michael and Buster fly to Iraq to spring their brother from prison.
insight: Season 3's weakest arc finale relies on character quirks over plot coherence and momentum.
